embed.alt: High-energy comedy trailer for “St. Paddy’s Beer Crawl.” Set in Chicago on St. Patrick’s Day—establishing aerial of the neon-green river, packed streets flooded with people in green. Smash cut into a rapid montage: a group of friends sprinting out of a crowded Irish pub, marking off a crumpled “ultimate crawl list.” Quick edits escalate the chaos—pints slamming on tables, one friend losing their balance mid-toast, another pulled into a loud singalong, someone spinning in a blur of green beads, door-to-door pub hopping. Handheld camera, whip pans, motion blur, bursts of slow-motion foam splashes, flashing bar lights. Dialogue layered over a driving Irish punk soundtrack: “How many stops left?” / “Too many!” / “We finish this!” Pace accelerates—sunset to night in flashes, faces getting more exhausted but determined. Final shot: the group staring at one last glowing pub entrance. Smash cut to black. Title card hits hard: “ST. PADDY’S BEER CRAWL.”

images.alt:
High-quality stylized 3D CGI Pixar-style render, vertical 3:4 composition, the iconic characters from The X-Files captured in a chaotic, playful bathroom mirror selfie; Fox Mulder holds a chunky 90s vintage camera toward the mirror while Dana Scully leans into frame with a skeptical raised eyebrow; Walter Skinner stands stiffly in the background trying to stay serious, while The Smoking Man lurks mysteriously at the edge of the reflection with a cigarette; the characters are tightly squeezed together with classic crowded selfie energy, each showing their signature expression—Mulder curious and excited, Scully analytical and unimpressed.
Everyone wears their most recognizable outfits faithful to the series (Mulder’s FBI suit and loosened tie, Scully’s blazer and cross necklace, Skinner’s stern suit, Smoking Man in shadow). Facial proportions are gently stylized in expressive Disney/Pixar animation style with large emotive eyes while preserving each character’s recognizable facial features, hairstyles, and personalities.
The bathroom mirror has subtle toothpaste splatters, condensation, and fingerprint smudges; written in the foggy glass in bold Pixar-style black lettering: “THE X-FILES ❤️”. The bathroom environment subtly reflects the show’s conspiracy-theory world: FBI badges on the counter, scattered case files, alien sketches, a “I Want to Believe” poster partly visible in the reflection, mysterious evidence bags, and a tiny alien figurine as an easter egg.
Lighting combines dim, moody bathroom lighting with a strong camera flash bouncing off the mirror, creating cinematic reflections and specular highlights; slightly mysterious teal-green and warm tungsten color contrast inspired by the show’s atmospheric tone; smooth Pixar-quality shading, soft volumetric haze, cinematic depth of field, ultra-detailed 4K render, polished Disney/Pixar-style character design, no watermark

National Lacy Oatmeal Cookie Day

images.alt:
Lacy oatmeal cookies are a type of cookie made from rolled oats, flour, sugar, butter, and other ingredients. They are known for their thin and delicate texture, which is achieved by spreading the batter thinly on a baking sheet before baking.
To make lacy oatmeal cookies, the ingredients are mixed together to form a batter, which is then dropped by spoonfuls onto a baking sheet. The batter is then spread out thinly to create a lace-like pattern when baked. The cookies are typically baked until they are golden brown and slightly crispy.
Lacy oatmeal cookies are often flavored with ingredients such as cinnamon, vanilla, or almond extract. Some recipes may also include chopped nuts or dried fruit. They are a popular snack or dessert item and can be enjoyed on their own or served with tea or coffee.
Lacy oatmeal cookies are a variation on traditional oatmeal cookies, which are typically thicker and chewier. The thin and delicate texture of lacy oatmeal cookies makes them a unique and elegant option for special occasions or as a treat with afternoon tea.

National Sloppy Joe Day

images.alt:
A sloppy joe is a sandwich made with ground beef or turkey that is cooked with onions, bell peppers, and tomato sauce, and typically served on a hamburger bun. The filling is often seasoned with a blend of spices, including chili powder, paprika, and garlic powder.
The origins of the sloppy joe sandwich are uncertain, but it is believed to have originated in the United States in the early 20th century. It was a popular dish in school cafeterias and home kitchens throughout the mid-20th century, and remains a favorite comfort food for many people today.
The name "sloppy joe" is said to have come from the fact that the sandwich can be messy to eat, with the saucy filling spilling out of the bun. However, this messy quality is also part of what makes the sandwich so beloved.
Sloppy joes can be customized to suit different tastes and preferences. Some people add cheese or pickles to their sandwiches, while others prefer to make the filling with ground turkey or even vegetarian substitutes. The sandwich can be served with a variety of sides, such as french fries, coleslaw, or potato salad.
